tribe.definition: a group of people who share the same beliefs or way of living
tribe.example: What is your parent tribe — are you a regulator or a facilitator?

seminal.definition: very important and influential, having a big effect on what comes after
seminal.example: The seminal book on parenting types was published thirty years ago.

regulators.definition: parents who prefer set routines and schedules for their children
regulators.example: Regulators like to keep the same daily routine.

facilitators.definition: parents who follow the child's lead rather than setting fixed routines
facilitators.example: Facilitators respond to what the baby seems to need.

adult-centric.definition: focused on the needs and preferences of the adult
adult-centric.example: Regulators tend to be more adult-centric in their approach.

routine-led.definition: organized around a set schedule of activities
routine-led.example: A routine-led approach means doing the same thing at the same time.

child-centric.definition: focused on the needs and feelings of the child
child-centric.example: Facilitators take a child-centric approach to parenting.

infant.example: Facilitators go with the flow of the infant.

philosophy.definition: a set of beliefs about how to live or do something
philosophy.example: Each parent has their own parenting philosophy.

predictability.definition: the quality of being expected and reliable, not surprising
predictability.example: Both types of parents believe predictability is important.

predictable.definition: happening in a way you can expect
predictable.example: A predictable response helps the baby feel safe.

cues.definition: signals or signs that tell you something
cues.example: The baby's cues are responded to by the facilitator parent.

leaning.definition: a preference or tendency toward something
leaning.example: You will have a leaning towards being one type or the other.

fluid.definition: not fixed, able to change easily
fluid.example: The roles of regulator and facilitator are fluid.

lob.definition: to throw something in a careless way
lob.example: It is not helpful to lob facts at each other to prove your point.

statistics.definition: numbers and data used to understand or prove something
statistics.example: Quoting statistics will not help you agree with your partner.

entrenched.definition: stuck firmly in a position and unwilling to change
entrenched.example: Admitting your feelings may help you become less entrenched.

inclined.definition: having a tendency or preference to do something
inclined.example: Whichever philosophy you are more inclined to follow, be kind.

acceptance.definition: the willingness to receive and be okay with something
acceptance.example: Acceptance and warmth matter most with children.

warmth.definition: a feeling of kindness, care, and friendliness
warmth.example: Warmth and kindness are what matter most.

upheaval.definition: a big change that causes confusion or trouble
upheaval.example: Facilitators tend to give in to the emotional upheaval of pregnancy.

inward-looking.definition: focused on your inner world, thoughts, and feelings
inward-looking.example: The facilitator becomes more inward-looking during pregnancy.

marvelling.example: She spent time marvelling at the baby growing inside her.

unfurling.definition: opening up and spreading out, like a flower blooming
unfurling.example: She marvelled at the wonder unfurling inside her.

persona.definition: the image or character you show to the world
persona.example: The regulator wants to cling to her normal persona.

altered.definition: changed from what it was before
altered.example: She tries not to give in to her altered state during pregnancy.

invasive.definition: entering where it is not wanted, intrusive
invasive.example: A regulator may feel the pregnancy as invasive.

enhanced.example: A facilitator feels her identity is enhanced by pregnancy.

threatened.definition: feeling in danger or at risk
threatened.example: A regulator may feel her identity is threatened by pregnancy.

transition.definition: a change from one state or stage to another
transition.example: Facilitators see birth as a mutual transition for mother and baby.

merely.example: A regulator may see birth merely as a painful event.

normalize.definition: to make something seem normal and acceptable
normalize.example: I mention these differences to help normalize your feelings.

spectrum.definition: a range between two opposite points
spectrum.example: Your peers may be on the opposite side of the spectrum to you.

lonely.definition: feeling sad because you are alone or different from others
lonely.example: Being different from your parenting peers can feel lonely.

directives.definition: instructions or orders telling you what to do
directives.example: There are many directives telling you the right way to parent.

acknowledging.definition: accepting that something is true or real
acknowledging.example: Acknowledging your feelings is the most important thing.

inclinations.definition: natural tendencies to do or think in a certain way
inclinations.example: Acknowledge your natural inclinations about parenting.

expectant.definition: waiting for a baby to be born, about to become a parent
expectant.example: This exercise is for expectant mothers and fathers.

anxious.definition: feeling worried and nervous about something
anxious.example: Are you feeling anxious about becoming a parent?

expectations.definition: things you believe will happen or should happen
expectations.example: Notice what expectations you have about becoming a parent.

tricked.definition: made to believe something false, fooled
tricked.example: Children should not be tricked into behaving well.

manipulating.definition: controlling someone cleverly in an unfair way
manipulating.example: Think about relating to children rather than manipulating them.

visualize.definition: to picture something in your mind
visualize.example: Visualize your body becoming familiar to your baby.

co-parenting.definition: raising a child together with a partner
co-parenting.example: If you are co-parenting, discuss your feelings together.

rupture.definition: a sudden break or tear in something
rupture.example: Do not believe you have caused a rupture that cannot be repaired.

concentration.definition: the ability to focus your mind on one thing
concentration.example: There may be no reason you caused your child's concentration problems.

environmental.definition: related to the conditions around you that affect your life
environmental.example: There may be no environmental reason why your child is the way they are.

berating.example: Berating yourself will not help anyone.

hormones.definition: chemicals in your body that affect how you feel and grow
hormones.example: Stress in pregnancy can happen because of hormones.
